title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Algorithmic Curation on Culture

texts.A: The decisive and largely irreversible transition from human editorial judgment and cultural curation to mathematical recommendation algorithms represents the final and perhaps most damaging blow to cultural serendipity—those unexpected, transformative encounters with the genuinely unfamiliar that have historically driven both personal growth and social progress. When a streaming platform or social media feed shows us exclusively what it algorithmically calculates we already enjoy, based solely on analysing our past behavioral patterns, it creates a powerful self-reinforcing feedback loop that systematically prevents the discovery of new ideas, challenging art, and alternative perspectives. We are no longer being genuinely challenged or productively unsettled by difficult or unfamiliar creative work; we are being relentlessly pampered by a sophisticated digital system that anticipates our every preference and never dares to truly surprise or disturb us. [38]This seductive comfort comes at a considerable cultural price: the gradual but inexorable death of the shared cultural conversation and common reference points that once gave coherence to a diverse and complex democratic society.[/38]
texts.B: To argue confidently that algorithmic recommendation systems 'stifle' genuine cultural discovery is to uncritically romanticize a historical past in which a small and socially homogeneous elite of professional critics, editors, and institutional gatekeepers unilaterally determined what creative work deserved public attention or commercial distribution. [37]Algorithmic curation is, at its democratic best, fundamentally meritocratic in its operational logic; it surfaces and elevates content based on measurable actual human engagement rather than the arbitrary personal preferences or social connections of a single magazine editor operating in a particular cultural context.[/37] [39]For the very first time in the history of mass media, genuinely niche artists and highly specialized creators can reliably find and cultivate their natural global audience without requiring the costly and selective endorsement of a powerful corporate intermediary.[/39] The widespread belief that users are simply 'trapped' in impenetrable filter bubbles significantly exaggerates the problem; most contemporary users are demonstrably exposed to a considerably wider variety of global content than audiences ever encountered in the narrowly constrained era of broadcast television.
texts.C: [38]I agree that the genuine erosion of cultural serendipity represents a legitimate and serious concern, but the primary blame lies with the specific profit-maximizing business models governing these platforms rather than with the underlying recommendation technology itself.[/38] Algorithms are deliberately and systematically engineered to maximize 'time on site' and advertising exposure, which inevitably promotes sensationalist, emotionally provocative, or simply repetitive content over more challenging, nuanced, or edifying material. However, [39]I genuinely share the view that this same technology has demonstrably and significantly empowered independent creators and enabled them to build sustainable, loyal audiences previously beyond their reach.[/39] The fundamental problem is not that algorithms exist and actively curate content, but that they remain deliberately opaque to users and entirely immune to meaningful public scrutiny or independent oversight. [40]If we could successfully demand genuine 'algorithmic transparency,' allowing users to meaningfully adjust their own discovery and recommendation parameters, we could enjoy the real benefits of personalization without the well-documented risks of the ideological echo chamber.[/40]
texts.D: The most profound and lasting danger of algorithmic content curation is not simply what it chooses to show individual users, but what it insidiously does to the creative producers themselves and to the broader cultural ecosystem they inhabit. Writers, musicians, filmmakers, and journalists are now systematically compelled to 'reverse-engineer' and compromise their work to satisfy an opaque and constantly shifting algorithmic code, producing an increasingly formulaic and predictable creative output that ruthlessly prioritizes easily measurable engagement metrics over artistic integrity, genuine risk-taking, and meaningful innovation. [37]While some describe this as a new democratization of cultural production, it is in practice a new and particularly insidious form of creative tyranny—the tyranny of the engagement metric and the algorithmic average of public preference.[/37] [40]I also firmly disagree that simple platform transparency represents a sufficient or adequate solution; as long as profit maximization remains the overriding institutional goal, algorithms will consistently and inevitably favor the addictive and the sensational over the genuinely transformative and the culturally enriching.[/40]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on whether algorithmic curation is democratic?
questions.explanation: Expert B claims algorithmic curation is 'fundamentally democratic.' Expert D disagrees, calling it a 'new form of tyranny' and the 'tyranny of the average.'

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the loss of cultural challenge or 'serendipity'?
questions.explanation: Expert A laments the 'coffin for cultural serendipity.' Expert C agrees, stating that the 'death of serendipity' is a 'legitimate concern.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the opportunities for independent or niche creators?
questions.explanation: Expert B says 'niche artists can find their audience without a middleman.' Expert C also shares the view that the technology has 'empowered independent creators.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the effectiveness of making algorithms more transparent?
questions.explanation: Expert C believes 'algorithmic transparency' would solve the problem. Expert D disagrees, stating that 'transparency is [not] a cure-all' as long as the goal remains profit.
